title: Inside the Musk-Altman Trial: A Legal Battle That Could Reshape the AI Industry
summary: A bitter, yearslong feud between two of the most powerful figures in artificial intelligence is finally heading to a courtroom. This week in Northern California, Elon Musk will face off against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman in a trial that could determine the future of the company behind ChatGPT—and potentially reshape the entire AI landscape. Musk, who co-founded OpenAI in 2015 as a nonprofit dedicated to developing AI for the public good, alleges that Altman and president Greg Brockman deceived him into bankrolling the venture. According to the lawsuit, they promised to keep the company a nonprofit, only to later restructure it with a for-profit subsidiary that now seeks an IPO. Musk is seeking up to $134 billion in damages—though he has asked the court to award any recovery to OpenAI's nonprofit arm rather than himself. He also wants Altman and Brockman ousted from their leadership roles. The trial promises to be a spectacle. Nine jurors will deliver an advisory verdict to guide the judge, and the witness list reads like a who's who of tech: Altman, Brockman, Musk, former chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, former CTO Mira Murati, and Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella are all expected to take the stand. Cringey texts, raw diary entries, and behind-the-scenes scheming from OpenAI's early days are likely to surface. "This lawsuit has always been a baseless and jealous bid to derail a competitor," an OpenAI spokesperson said, while Musk has posted on X that "Scam Altman lies as easily as he breathes." At the heart of the case is a thorny legal question: can a donor sue a nonprofit for changing its structure? Legal scholars are puzzled. "The idea that Elon Musk can sue because he was a donor or used to be on the board is pretty puzzling," says Jill Horwitz, a law professor at Northwestern University. Typically, only state attorneys general can enforce charitable purposes—and in October 2025, the attorneys general of California and Delaware already struck a deal with OpenAI to approve its new corporate structure with conditions, including a safety committee. Critics, including Musk, argue those safeguards are insufficient. Rose Chan Loui of UCLA School of Law notes that Musk must show "what the deficiencies are" in that deal. Further complicating matters, experts say the court is analyzing the case under trust law, but OpenAI is a corporation, not a trust—potentially applying the wrong legal framework. The stakes are enormous. OpenAI, valued at over $850 billion, has warned that the litigation poses a risk to its business. Musk's rival company xAI, which makes the chatbot Grok, is expected to go public as part of SpaceX as early as June, with a combined valuation of $1.25 trillion. A victory for Musk could cripple OpenAI just as it races to IPO, handing a massive advantage to xAI. Regardless of the outcome, the trial will offer a rare, unfiltered look into the secretive world of AI development and the personal animosities driving it.
why_it_matters: The trial could decide who controls the future of tools like ChatGPT, reminding you that even cutting-edge technology is shaped by human rivalries and legal battles.

keywords.explanation: An organization that uses its money to pursue a public mission rather than to generate profits for owners.
A business unit created by a nonprofit to earn money, while the parent organization still has a charitable goal.
Initial Public Offering; the first sale of a company's shares to the public, turning it into a stock-market traded corporation.
The chief legal officers of a state or country, responsible for enforcing laws and protecting the public interest.
A jury's non-binding opinion that helps a judge decide a case but does not automatically determine the final outcome.
A set of legal rules that governs how one party manages property or assets for the benefit of another, often used for charities.

questions.question: What is Elon Musk's main allegation in the lawsuit?
options: Altman and Brockman deceived him into funding a nonprofit that later became for-profit
Musk wants to take control of the board
questions.correct_answer: Altman and Brockman deceived him into funding a nonprofit that later became for-profit

questions.question: How much in damages is Musk seeking?
Which Microsoft figure is expected to testify?
What legal framework are experts saying the court is incorrectly applying?
Which two states' attorneys general already approved OpenAI's new corporate structure with conditions?
What is the name of Musk's AI company that makes the chatbot Grok?

background_read: In 2015, Elon Musk and Sam Altman co-founded OpenAI as a nonprofit artificial intelligence research company with the stated mission of ensuring that AI benefits all of humanity. Musk, who at the time also led Tesla and SpaceX, contributed tens of millions of dollars. However, in 2018, Musk left OpenAI's board, reportedly after a power struggle over its direction.

The following year, OpenAI created a 'capped-profit' subsidiary, OpenAI LP, to attract investment while limiting returns for investors. Microsoft, the tech giant, soon invested billions, gaining early access to OpenAI's models. By 2023, OpenAI's ChatGPT had become a global sensation, and the company moved toward a full for-profit structure to support its enormous computing costs. Musk, meanwhile, founded xAI in 2023, a rival AI lab whose chatbot Grok is integrated with the social platform X (formerly Twitter).

The attorneys general of California and Delaware—the top legal officials responsible for enforcing charitable laws in those states—reviewed OpenAI's restructuring and approved it in late 2025, imposing safeguards like a safety committee. Yet Musk argues those safeguards are insufficient and that Altman and president Greg Brockman (a co-founder who previously worked with Musk at Stripe) deceived him.

Legal experts find the case puzzling because, under American law, individual donors typically lack the right to sue over a nonprofit's structural changes; that authority belongs to state attorneys general. The trial will feature an advisory jury and testimony from witnesses including Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella (who oversees the company's multi-billion-dollar partnership with OpenAI) and former chief scientist Ilya Sutskever (who co-founded OpenAI and later left to start a new AI safety company).
Article_Structure: LEAD: 'A bitter years-long feud... heading to a courtroom.'
├─ PARTIES: Elon Musk vs. Sam Altman, Greg Brockman
└─ STAKES: could reshape AI industry
LAWSUIT CLAIMS
├─ Musk: deceived into funding nonprofit, later restructured for-profit
├─ SEEKING: $134 billion damages (awarded to nonprofit), removal of leaders
└─ OpenAI response: 'baseless and jealous bid to derail a competitor'
TRIAL SPECTACLE
├─ JURY & WITNESSES: nine jurors, tech who's who (Nadella, Sutskever, etc.)
└─ EVIDENCE: cringey texts, diary entries, early scheming
LEGAL COMPLEXITY
├─ CORE QUESTION: can a donor sue a nonprofit for changing structure?
├─ SCHOLAR VIEW: unusual, typically only state attorneys general enforce
└─ DEAL: CA and DE AGs already approved structure with safeguards
CRITICAL FLAW: court applying trust law to corporation?
└─ Expert: Rose Chan Loui notes Musk must show deficiencies in deal
STAKES & MOTIVATIONS
├─ OpenAI: valued $850B+, warns litigation risk
└─ Musk's xAI: expected to go public with SpaceX, $1.25T valuation
OPEN QUESTION: victory for Musk could cripple OpenAI, advantage xAI

perspectives.description: OpenAI supporters argue the for-profit shift was necessary to secure the billions needed to develop transformative AI, ultimately benefiting the public.
Musk's side contends that a nonprofit mission was betrayed for private gain, potentially undermining public trust in AI safety pledges.
The legal oddity of a donor suing a corporation under trust law exposes gaps in how we govern hybrid nonprofit/for-profit tech entities.
Regardless of the verdict, the case could prompt new regulations clarifying how AI companies can balance mission and profit, and whether founders' early promises are legally binding.
